templates.title: Early feeding rhythm
templates.feedingFocus: Keep milk feeds responsive and watch for hunger, fullness, and comfort cues.
templates.developmentFocus: Notice short alert windows, burping comfort, and whether recovery after feeds is getting easier.
safetyReminders: Hold the baby upright after feeds when spit-up or reflux is a concern.
Use paced bottle feeds when bottles are part of the routine.
Keep sleep surfaces clear and avoid feeding-position props.
playSuggestions: Use brief face-to-face time during calm wake windows.
Talk through diaper changes so the baby hears your voice often.
routineFocus: Note which parts of the day have the smoothest feeding rhythm.
Track comfort cues so cluster-feeding days are easier to recognize.
commonQuestions: Is cluster feeding normal right now?
How do I tell hunger from comfort sucking?

templates.title: Steadier milk routine
templates.feedingFocus: Keep milk feeds steady and notice whether longer stretches between feeds still match the baby's cues.
templates.developmentFocus: Watch head control, longer wake windows, and whether the baby stays settled after feeds.
safetyReminders: Pause to burp when the baby gets gulping or fussy mid-feed.
Keep bottle nipples and pump parts matched to the baby's pace, not just age labels.
Do not prop bottles during feeds.
playSuggestions: Try short tummy-time sessions after a comfortable post-feed break.
Use simple songs or mirrored facial expressions during alert time.
routineFocus: Look for a repeatable feed-play-rest rhythm, even if it is still loose.
Notice which feeds are easiest and which ones need more pacing.
commonQuestions: Is my baby ready for a more predictable schedule?
What does distracted feeding look like at this age?

templates.title: Solids readiness watch
templates.feedingFocus: Keep milk as the main intake while watching for steady head control, supported sitting, and interest in food.
templates.developmentFocus: Notice oral readiness, hand-to-mouth coordination, and whether the baby can stay upright with support.
safetyReminders: Wait for clear readiness signs before moving into solids.
Use upright supported seating for any tasting practice.
Avoid putting cereal or purees into bottles.
playSuggestions: Offer texture play with spoons, cups, or teething-safe tools outside meals.
Let the baby watch family meals to build interest without pressure.
routineFocus: Decide which time of day would be the calmest for first-solid practice later.
Keep milk logs clear so solids do not crowd out the main intake too early.
commonQuestions: How do I know the baby is truly ready for solids?
Should we wait if sitting support is still inconsistent?

templates.title: First-solid variety
templates.feedingFocus: Build slow variety with iron-rich foods, produce, and familiar milk feeds still carrying most intake.
templates.developmentFocus: Notice chewing motions, tongue control, and how the baby handles new textures across repeated tries.
safetyReminders: Introduce textures that match the baby's oral skills and upright posture.
Offer one higher-risk allergen at a time when possible so reactions are easier to track.
Avoid hard round foods and other obvious choking hazards.
playSuggestions: Let the baby hold a spoon or soft finger food for sensory practice.
Model chewing and exaggerated mouth movements during meals.
routineFocus: Repeat new foods several times before deciding they are disliked.
Log reactions, textures, and successful meal times so the pattern is easier to review.
commonQuestions: How many solids should happen in a day right now?
What should I do when one texture goes well and another does not?

templates.title: Texture and self-feeding practice
templates.feedingFocus: Expand finger foods, repeated iron-rich meals, and mixed textures while the baby practices self-feeding.
templates.developmentFocus: Watch pincer grasp, chewing endurance, cup practice, and how the baby manages more table-style foods.
safetyReminders: Keep food pieces soft enough to mash and easy to grasp.
Stay close during self-feeding because gagging and mess still come with learning.
Cut round or slippery foods into safer shapes.
playSuggestions: Offer safe scooping and cup-holding practice during meals.
Use naming games for foods, utensils, and family mealtime routines.
routineFocus: Build repeated meal times so texture practice happens without rushing.
Keep one reliable iron-rich option in frequent rotation.
commonQuestions: How much self-feeding practice is enough before frustration sets in?
When should cup practice start replacing bottles at the table?

templates.title: Family-meal transition
templates.feedingFocus: Shift toward family meals, predictable snacks, and letting the toddler practice independence without losing variety.
templates.developmentFocus: Notice appetite swings, utensil practice, and how mealtime behavior changes with routine or tiredness.
safetyReminders: Keep supervising because choking risk is still real with fast eating or overstuffing.
Watch milk volume so it does not replace meals.
Seat the toddler for meals and snacks instead of walking around with food.
playSuggestions: Let the toddler help with simple meal setup like carrying a spoon or napkin.
Use pretend feeding play with dolls or stuffed animals to reinforce routines.
routineFocus: Protect a regular meal-snack rhythm so grazing does not take over.
Keep offering foods that were previously accepted even after refusal streaks.
commonQuestions: Is this picky eating or a normal toddler appetite swing?
How much milk is too much once meals are established?

templates.title: Routine and variety reinforcement
templates.feedingFocus: Reinforce consistent meals, snack boundaries, and enough variety to keep protein, produce, and iron-rich foods visible.
templates.developmentFocus: Notice autonomy, opinion-driven refusals, and whether routines help the toddler stay calmer at the table.
safetyReminders: Keep high-risk textures modified even if chewing seems strong.
Stay consistent about seated meals and supervised snacks.
Avoid pressure or bargaining that turns meals into battles.
playSuggestions: Use simple sorting or color games with foods during prep, not during pressure moments at the table.
Invite the toddler to help wash produce or place items on a plate.
routineFocus: Keep one or two dependable foods on the plate while still rotating exposure foods.
Log longer refusal streaks or reactions so review pages stay useful.
commonQuestions: How do I respond to repeated refusal without making it worse?
What matters more right now: total volume or food variety?

templates.title: Toddler habits and observation
templates.feedingFocus: Use steady family-meal routines and keep reminders focused on variety, independence, and observation over time.
templates.developmentFocus: Notice language around food, stronger preferences, and whether routines still support calm meals and snacks.
safetyReminders: Keep choking-risk foods modified and meals supervised.
Avoid using screens or bribes as the default tool for eating.
Keep hydration and stool patterns in mind when appetite changes.
playSuggestions: Use pretend grocery, cooking, or color-sorting games to make foods familiar.
Let the toddler participate in simple prep steps that do not add pressure.
routineFocus: Protect a predictable meal-snack structure with enough gaps for hunger to build.
Keep rotating familiar produce and protein options so variety does not narrow.
commonQuestions: How much independence should I allow at meals right now?
When does a narrow food list deserve closer review?
